title: Cat Head Pressing: Emergency Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Cat head pressing is a serious neurological emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is required and how to identify this critical behavior.
content_html:
TL;DR: Head pressing is a critical medical emergency signaling serious neurological damage; seek immediate veterinary care if your cat compulsively presses their head against objects.

What is head pressing in cats and how can I recognize it?

Head pressing is a compulsive behavior where a cat pushes their head against a wall, corner, or another firm object for an extended period. This behavior is distinct from normal behavior in several ways:

  • It is different from affectionate head butting or "bunting."
  • The cat often appears distressed, dazed, or unresponsive.
  • The pressure applied is constant and serves no obvious purpose.

Is head pressing a medical emergency for my cat?

Yes, head pressing is a high-priority medical emergency indicating a serious neurological condition or central nervous system damage. Potential causes include:

  • Metabolic disorders such as liver failure.
  • Toxic exposure like lead poisoning.
  • Brain tumors.
  • Head trauma.

You should seek immediate veterinary attention at an emergency clinic without delay.

Why should I provide a photo or video of the behavior to the veterinary team?

If it is safe to do so, taking a quick video or photo of your cat's behavior can be incredibly beneficial for the triage process because:

  • Cats often hide symptoms when they are stressed at the clinic.
  • Clear visuals allow the veterinarian to confirm the symptom quickly.
  • It helps the vet assess the severity of the neurological state and move faster toward life-saving treatment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

During triage of emergency patients, several historical or observed problems warrant immediate transfer to the treatment area. Severe alterations in mental state, such as head pressing, seizures, or loss of consciousness, are critical. Rapid detection of cardiopulmonary arrest (CPA) is essential; lack of response to external stimuli or limp body tone are unreliable indicators. Owners should be instructed to watch for chest excursions and to touch the cornea or eyelids to elicit a corneal or palpebral reflex in an unconscious pet, with absence of one or both indicative of CPA. If the animal is cyanotic and collapsed (and was pawing at the face), an upper airway obstruction may be present; the owner should be instructed how to perform a Heimlich maneuver or sudden chest compression to relieve the obstruction and then to manually clear the airway.

Chapter: Emergency, Toxicology, Surgery, Cardiology, Pulmonology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1660)

faqs.question: Can I wait a few hours to see if my cat stops head pressing?
faqs.answer: No. Head pressing indicates a critical malfunction of the nervous system. Delaying care could lead to permanent damage or be life-threatening.

faqs.question: Are there other symptoms I should look for?
faqs.answer: Yes, you may also notice pacing, circling in one direction, changes in vision, seizures, or sudden changes in personality or behavior.

faqs.question: Is head pressing the same as rubbing their head on me?
faqs.answer: No. Rubbing against you is a social behavior used to mark scent. Head pressing is a persistent, static behavior against a hard surface, often accompanied by a blank stare.
